method.description: Hierarchical clustering over pairwise species similarity, followed by cluster-level summaries from the species feature matrix.
method.cluster_caveat: Clusters should be interpreted as exploratory seasonal assemblages rather than fixed ecological categories.

linkage.node_id_convention: Leaf nodes are 0..n_species-1 in species_input_order; internal nodes are n_species..2*n_species-2 in linkage row order.

clusters.description: Single-species cluster containing Snowberry, mainly representing winter visitor with autumn arrival component. The defining pattern is a winter peak around September, a moderate autumn component, moderate summer suppression, and balanced response dynamics. Its defining traits include year wrapping winter presence, atypical winter peak, and moderate autumn component. Compared with the full species set, trough month is lower than the whole-set average and autumn to winter weight ratio is higher than the whole-set average.

Cluster of 6 species, mainly representing resident with summer detectability collapse. Detectability peaks around May and and is lowest around September. The shared pattern includes moderate baseline presence, moderate summer suppression, weak autumn component, and rapid decline biased response dynamics. Common high-support traits include resident detectability pattern, moderate summer suppression, and late spring early summer detectability peak. Compared with the full species set, fit score is higher than the whole-set average.

Cluster of 2 species, mainly representing narrow winter seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from January to March, with a mean peak around February, and and an average width of 1.7 months. It is characterised by narrow season and moderate active window. Common high-support traits include winter peak, narrow season, and strong offseason suppression. Compared with the full species set, season end month is lower than the whole-set average and season midpoint month is lower than the whole-set average.

Cluster of 8 species, mainly representing extended spring seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from April to October, with a mean peak around May, and and an average width of 6.0 months. It is characterised by broad season and moderate active window. Common high-support traits include spring peak, strong post peak decline, and early peak alignment.

Cluster of 7 species, mainly representing extended summer seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from May to November, with a mean peak around July, and and an average width of 5.7 months. It is characterised by very broad season and moderate active window. Common high-support traits include summer peak, strong offseason suppression, and strong post peak decline.

Cluster of 4 species, mainly representing moderate summer seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from May to September, with a mean peak around July, and and an average width of 3.5 months. It is characterised by moderate season and moderate active window. Common high-support traits include summer peak, central peak alignment, and moderate seasonal window.

Cluster of 5 species, mainly representing moderate spring seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from April to June, with a mean peak around May, and and an average width of 2.6 months. It is characterised by moderate season and sharp active window. Common high-support traits include spring peak, moderate season, and strong offseason suppression. Compared with the full species set, season end month is lower than the whole-set average.

Cluster of 2 species, mainly representing narrow spring seasonal presence. The fitted active window runs roughly from May to June, with a mean peak around May, and and an average width of 1.1 months. It is characterised by narrow season and sharp active window. Common high-support traits include spring peak, narrow season, and moderate offseason suppression. Compared with the full species set, season width months is lower than the whole-set average and season end month is lower than the whole-set average.
